Title: Metabolic Engineering of Fatty Acid Biosynthesis in Plants

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Laurate-specific coconut LPAAT increased laurate accumulation in rapeseed seed oil by enabling sn-2 incorporation, raising total laurate to 67%.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Endogenous rapeseed TAG assembly poorly incorporated laurate at the sn-2 position. 2. Introduction of a laurate-specific coconut LPAAT into rapeseed already expressing California bay thioesterase increased laurate to 67% of seed fatty acids. 3. The result showed acyltransferase specificity is a major determinant of unusual fatty acid accumulation in TAG.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: transgenic coexpression in rapeseed; TAG positional analysis; seed fatty acid profiling
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful stacking gene for high-laurate oil breeding/engineering; should be combined with medium-chain thioesterases and possibly other TAG assembly enzymes.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Acts in storage lipid assembly rather than de novo fatty acid synthesis; highlighted as necessary for efficient incorporation of unusual fatty acids into TAG.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributed to wax ester production in Arabidopsis seeds when coexpressed with elongase and wax synthase.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Jojoba acyl-CoA reductase reduces elongated acyl-CoAs to fatty alcohols for wax biosynthesis. 2. Coordinated expression with elongase and wax synthase produced waxes up to 70% of seed oil in Arabidopsis. 3. The study indicated the introduced set of genes was sufficient for high wax accumulation.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: gene cloning; coordinated transgenic expression in Arabidopsis; seed lipid profiling
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Key component for engineering industrial wax-producing oilseeds; should be stacked with elongase and wax synthase.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: This review attributes cloning to Metz et al. 2000 and Lardizabal et al. 2000.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enabled final esterification step for high wax accumulation in Arabidopsis seeds when coexpressed with elongase and reductase.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Jojoba wax synthase esterifies fatty alcohols with fatty acyl-CoAs to form wax esters. 2. Coexpression with elongase and acyl-CoA reductase in Arabidopsis enabled waxes to reach 70% of seed oil. 3. The result suggested that the principal genes required for seed wax production had been identified.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: gene cloning; coordinated transgenic expression in Arabidopsis; seed lipid profiling
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Central engineering target for creating wax-accumulating crops for lubricants and cosmetics; requires pathway stacking.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Terminal assembly enzyme for jojoba-like seed wax biosynthesis.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Overexpression increased seed oil content and seed weight in Arabidopsis.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Overexpression of Arabidopsis diacylglycerol acyltransferase increased flux into TAG. 2. This increased seed oil content and seed weight. 3. The finding supports late TAG assembly as an effective engineering target for raising oil accumulation.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: transgenic overexpression in Arabidopsis; seed oil quantification; seed weight analysis
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong target for increasing seed oil via enhanced sink strength, especially in combination with precursor-supply genes.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Discussed as evidence that late TAG biosynthesis may more effectively increase oil than overexpressing individual FAS enzymes.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Overexpression increased seed oil content in Arabidopsis and rapeseed, with field-trial increases reported in rapeseed.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Overexpression of a yeast long-chain sn-2 acyltransferase increased seed oil content by over 50% dry mass per seed in Arabidopsis and rapeseed. 2. Rapeseed field trials showed 8.1-13.5% increases. 3. This supports enhancement of TAG assembly sink strength as a route to increased oil accumulation.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: transgenic overexpression; seed oil quantification; field trials in rapeseed
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Promising engineering target to raise oil yield; should be evaluated with agronomic performance and combined with upstream supply traits.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Exact gene symbol not provided in review text.

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: plastid fatty acid synthesis / medium-chain fatty acid production
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: chain termination of medium-chain acyl-ACP intermediates
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydrolyzes medium-chain acyl-ACP, especially 12:0-ACP, to release free lauric acid and terminate plastid fatty acid elongation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Expression in Arabidopsis and rapeseed redirected flux to laurate, producing up to 24% and 58% of seed fatty acids, respectively.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. California bay thioesterase short-circuited acyl chain elongation in non-laurate plants. 2. Transgenic Arabidopsis and rapeseed accumulated high laurate in seed oil. 3. Efficient total accumulation still required compatible downstream acyltransferase activity for sn-2 incorporation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: transgenic seed-specific expression; seed fatty acid profiling; TAG positional analysis
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Foundational gene for high-laurate oilseed engineering; best used with laurate-specific LPAAT and other TAG assembly factors.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Position analysis showed laurate at sn-1 and sn-3 but not sn-2 before LPAAT stacking.

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: plastid fatty acid synthesis / short-chain fatty acid production
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: chain termination of short-chain acyl-ACP intermediates
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydrolyzes short/medium-chain acyl-ACP intermediates to release short-chain fatty acids and terminate elongation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Expression in rapeseed produced only 12% caprylate, far below donor species levels.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Cuphea thioesterase expression in rapeseed generated caprylate accumulation. 2. Product level was much lower than in Cuphea, indicating host limitations. 3. Low short-chain acyl-ACP pool availability was suggested as a key constraint.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: transgenic expression in rapeseed; seed fatty acid profiling
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Requires stacking with short-chain-specific KAS and likely acyltransferases to improve product yield.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review emphasizes that single-gene transfer was insufficient to match donor accumulation levels.

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Capric acid; Lauric acid; Palmitic acid; Stearic acid
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydrolyzes specific acyl-ACP intermediates from ACP to release free fatty acids of defined chain lengths.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: free fatty acids of target chain length
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Different FatB-type thioesterases increased specific saturated fatty acids, including palmitate and stearate, but often below donor-species levels.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. FatB thioesterases from multiple species were used to alter chain-length composition in transgenic oilseeds. 2. Arabidopsis and Cuphea-derived activities increased palmitate in Arabidopsis and rapeseed. 3. Mangosteen activity increased stearate in rapeseed, whereas elm and nutmeg genes gave only limited caprate/laurate accumulation in rapeseed.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: transgenic overexpression; seed fatty acid profiling
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for tailoring saturated-fatty-acid chain length but often needs precursor-supply and TAG assembly genes for high accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review groups multiple thioesterase examples rather than focusing on a single locus.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes condensation reactions with specificity toward 6:0- and 8:0-acyl-ACPs, increasing short-chain acyl-ACP intermediates.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: When combined with Cuphea thioesterases, increased short-chain fatty acid accumulation in transgenic rapeseed relative to single-transgene lines.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Cuphea KAS isoforms have unique specificity for short-chain acyl-ACP intermediates. 2. Crossing KAS-expressing lines with Cuphea thioesterase lines increased short-chain fatty acids. 3. The result demonstrates precursor supply strongly limits accumulation of engineered short-chain products.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: transgenic expression; genetic crossing; seed fatty acid profiling
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Important stacking partner for short-chain oil engineering to enlarge substrate pools for specialized thioesterases.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review notes multiple genes may be required to obtain commercially meaningful levels of short-chain fatty acids.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Glycine max; Brassica napus; Gossypium hirsutum
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Glycine max; Brassica sp.; Gossypium sp.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Introduces a Δ9 double bond into stearoyl-ACP to form oleoyl-ACP.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Down-regulation increased seed stearic acid levels up to 40% in Brassica, 38% in cotton, and contributed to stearate increases in soybean.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Seed-specific suppression of stearoyl-ACP desaturase raised stearate in multiple oilseeds. 2. This produced semisolid oils without hydrogenation. 3. The strategy was highlighted as beneficial for reducing trans-fat formation during food processing.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: cosuppression/antisense/RNA interference; seed fatty acid profiling
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for producing high-stearate oils for margarine-type applications without hydrogenation.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Suppression of this enzyme can be combined with Δ12 desaturase suppression in soybean.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Soybean; Brassica napus; Cotton; Brassica juncea; Arabidopsis
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Glycine max; Brassica napus; Gossypium hirsutum; Brassica juncea; Arabidopsis thaliana
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Glycine max; Brassica sp.; Gossypium sp.; Arabidopsis thaliana
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Introduces a Δ12 double bond into oleic acid to produce linoleic acid.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Suppression increased oleic acid to as high as 86-89% and reduced 18:2 to below 1% in soybean.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Down-regulation of oleoyl-Δ12 desaturase strongly increased oleic acid in several oilseed species. 2. In soybean, high-oleic oil became a commercial product with improved oxidative stability. 3. The modified oil is useful for frying and potential biodegradable lubricant applications.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: gene suppression; seed fatty acid profiling; commercial field deployment
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Major breeding and engineering target for high-oleic, oxidatively stable oils with food and industrial uses.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review links high-oleic oil to reduced need for hydrogenation and potential health benefits via altered omega-6/omega-3 balance.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Introduces a Δ6 double bond into suitable 18-carbon unsaturated fatty acids to form γ-linolenic acid or related intermediates.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: linoleic acid or oleoyl-derived precursor with Δ12 desaturation context
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Overexpression enabled γ-linolenic acid accumulation up to 47% in rapeseed and low levels in tobacco.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Heterologous Δ6 desaturase expression introduced nonnative γ-linolenic acid production in plants. 2. The highest reported level in this review was 47% in Brassica napus. 3. Performance depended on coexisting desaturation capacity and host context.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Valuable for producing nutritionally important polyunsaturated oils; may require pathway balancing with other desaturases.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Table pairs Δ6 and Δ12 desaturases for Mortierella-derived engineering in B. napus.

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: unusual fatty acid modification in ER lipids
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts unsaturated fatty acids into conjugated fatty acids with altered double-bond geometry.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Expression in soybean produced conjugated fatty acids up to 17% of total seed fatty acids.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Momordica conjugase conferred synthesis of conjugated fatty acids in soybean. 2. Target products reached 17% of seed fatty acids. 3. This demonstrated transferability of unusual fatty acid biosynthesis, though not yet to donor-species levels.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: transgenic overexpression; seed fatty acid profiling
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential route to industrial conjugated oils, likely requiring additional genes for efficient accumulation and TAG incorporation.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes condensation during acyl-CoA elongation to generate very-long-chain fatty acid intermediates leading to Δ5-eicosenoic acid.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: With meadowfoam acyl-CoA desaturase, enabled Δ5-eicosenoic acid accumulation up to 18% in soybean.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Meadowfoam β-ketoacyl-CoA synthase supported very-long-chain monoene biosynthesis in transgenic soybean. 2. Coengineering with acyl-CoA desaturase produced Δ5-eicosenoic acid. 3. Demonstrates combinatorial engineering of elongation plus desaturation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: transgenic coexpression; seed fatty acid profiling
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for engineering specialty very-long-chain monoenes for industrial applications.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Introduces a double bond into elongated acyl-CoA substrates to form Δ5-eicosenoic acid.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Coexpression with β-ketoacyl-CoA synthase produced Δ5-eicosenoic acid up to 18% in soybean.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Meadowfoam acyl-CoA desaturase functioned in soybean to generate a novel very-long-chain monoene. 2. Product accumulation required paired elongation and desaturation functions. 3. This illustrates pathway reconstruction for specialty oil biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: transgenic coexpression; seed fatty acid profiling
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful in specialty oil platforms with accompanying elongase genes.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates oleate at carbon 12 to generate ricinoleic/hydroxy fatty acids.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Expression in Arabidopsis generated hydroxy fatty acids up to 30% and ricinoleic acid up to 17%.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Castor and Lesquerella hydroxylases conferred hydroxy fatty acid synthesis in Arabidopsis. 2. Product levels reached 30% total hydroxy fatty acids. 3. These studies exemplify successful transfer of unusual ER-modifying enzymes to model oilseeds.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: transgenic overexpression; seed fatty acid profiling
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Promising for industrial hydroxy-oil production, but likely needs complementary assembly/editing genes for maximal accumulation.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Introduces an acetylenic bond into fatty acid substrates to form acetylenic fatty acids.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Expression in Arabidopsis produced acetylenic fatty acids up to 25%.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Crepis acetylenase enabled synthesis of acetylenic fatty acids in Arabidopsis. 2. Product reached 25% of seed fatty acids. 3. The case demonstrates engineering of highly unusual industrial fatty acid structures in plants.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: transgenic overexpression; seed fatty acid profiling
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate for specialty industrial oil development in oilseed crops.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Epoxidizes an unsaturated fatty acid substrate to generate epoxy fatty acids.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: oleic acid-derived unsaturated fatty acid substrate
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Expression in Arabidopsis produced epoxy fatty acid up to 15%.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Crepis epoxygenase conferred epoxy fatty acid production in Arabidopsis. 2. The engineered product reached 15% of seed fatty acids. 3. This establishes transfer of epoxy-fatty-acid biosynthesis into a model oilseed host.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: transgenic overexpression; seed fatty acid profiling
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential tool for industrial epoxy oil production; may require additional genes to improve flux and storage.

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: very-long-chain fatty acid elongation / wax biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Elongates oleate-derived acyl-CoAs to long-chain monounsaturated acyl-CoAs used for wax ester formation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: In combination with jojoba reductase and wax synthase, enabled wax accumulation up to 70% of seed oil in Arabidopsis.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Lunaria annua elongase generated very-long-chain acyl-CoA precursors for wax synthesis. 2. Triple-gene expression with jojoba reductase and acyltransferase reconstituted high wax production in Arabidopsis seeds. 3. The pathway was considered largely sufficient for the trait.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: coordinated transgenic expression; seed lipid profiling
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Required precursor-supply enzyme for engineering jojoba-like wax crops.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Introduces a double bond at the Δ4 position of palmitoyl-ACP to generate an unusual monoene associated with petroselinic-type products.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Expression in tobacco yielded low petroselinic acid accumulation (~4%), much less than donor species.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Coriander unusual acyl-ACP desaturase could function in heterologous plants. 2. Product accumulation remained low compared with native coriander seeds. 3. The review suggests cofactor specificity and specialized downstream lipid metabolism may constrain accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Illustrates potential for unusual monoene engineering but indicates need for additional species-specific supporting enzymes.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review discusses special involvement of coriander-specific KAS, thioesterase, and acyltransferase activities in native accumulation.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Carboxylates acetyl-CoA to form malonyl-CoA, the committed precursor for fatty acid synthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: seed-specific promoter for plastid-targeted homomeric ACCase; CaMV 35S used for BC subunit in tobacco
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Plastid-targeted homomeric ACCase increased ACCase activity and raised rapeseed oil content slightly (~3 percentage points dry weight), whereas overexpression of only the BC subunit in tobacco had no effect on fatty acids.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ACCase-catalyzed malonyl-CoA production is presented as a key regulatory and committed step in de novo fatty acid synthesis. 2. Seed-specific plastid-targeted homomeric ACCase in rapeseed increased oil yield modestly. 3. Overexpression of only the BC subunit failed to elevate total ACCase or fatty acids, showing multimeric enzyme coordination is necessary.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: transgenic overexpression; plastid targeting; enzyme activity assay; seed oil quantification
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful precursor-supply target, but alone gives limited gains; likely should be combined with other pathway or sink-strength genes.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review explicitly states the small gain indicates additional control points beyond ACCase.

Pathway_Genes.Enzyme_Name: Biotin carboxylase subunit of acetyl-CoA carboxylase
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Subunit of plastidial heteromeric ACCase involved in acetyl-CoA carboxylation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Threefold increase in BC protein in tobacco leaves did not increase other ACCase subunits or alter fatty acid content/composition.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Overexpression of a single ACCase subunit was insufficient to raise total ACCase function. 2. No change in fatty acid content or composition was observed in tobacco. 3. This highlighted the importance of coordinated accumulation of ACCase subunits.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: transgenic overexpression; protein accumulation analysis; fatty acid analysis
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Not effective as a stand-alone target; engineering should use complete ACCase strategies rather than single-subunit overexpression.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes condensation of acetyl-CoA with malonyl-ACP in the initial step of fatty acid synthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Overexpression decreased rather than increased fatty acid content in tobacco and rapeseed.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Strong overexpression of spinach KAS III in tobacco caused a 5-10% decrease in fatty acid content. 2. Cuphea KAS III expression in rapeseed embryos caused a 9% decrease. 3. The study concluded that increasing a single FAS enzyme does not necessarily increase flux and may perturb malonyl-CoA availability.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: transgenic overexpression; enzyme activity assay; fatty acid quantification
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Poor single-gene strategy for increasing oil; indicates need for coordinated pathway regulation instead of isolated enzyme upregulation.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Overexpression also increased ACP protein levels in tobacco, but other FAS activities were unaffected.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers malonyl from malonyl-CoA to ACP to form malonyl-ACP.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Targeting the E. coli enzyme to rapeseed leucoplasts increased activity up to 45-fold but did not increase fatty acid content.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Large increases in malonyl-CoA:ACP transacylase activity did not enhance fatty acid accumulation. 2. This supports the conclusion that single-enzyme up-regulation downstream of ACCase is insufficient to raise FAS flux. 3. Flux control in seed oil synthesis is distributed and complex.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: plastid-targeted transgenic expression; enzyme activity assay; fatty acid quantification
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Not recommended as a stand-alone engineering target for oil increase.
